Name: info_theoretic_compression
Title: Information-Theoretic Limits of Model Compression: A Rate-Distortion Approach
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the fundamental limits of model compression can be characterized using rate-distortion theory, which provides a quantitative framework for understanding the trade-off between compression ratio and model accuracy. By applying this framework to deep neural networks, we can identify the optimal compression strategies that achieve the best possible trade-off between model size and accuracy.
Related Work: Recent works such as EMQ, AMP-ViT, and SNIP have explored mixed-precision quantization and compression techniques for deep learning models. However, these approaches are largely heuristic and lack a rigorous information-theoretic foundation. In contrast, our approach is grounded in rate-distortion theory, which provides a principled framework for understanding the fundamental limits of model compression. The Bioelectrical Information Theory work also explores information-theoretic compression, but in the context of bioelectrical signals, whereas our work focuses on deep learning models.
Abstract: This paper proposes a novel approach to model compression based on rate-distortion theory. We formulate the compression problem as a rate-distortion optimization, where the goal is to minimize the compression ratio (rate) subject to a constraint on the model accuracy (distortion). We apply this framework to deep neural networks and derive theoretical bounds on the optimal compression ratio as a function of the model architecture and the desired accuracy. Our approach provides a principled and quantitative understanding of the trade-off between model size and accuracy, and can be used to guide the design of compression algorithms and techniques.
Experiments: We will conduct experiments on a range of deep learning models, including convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and transformers, to evaluate the effectiveness of our rate-distortion approach. We will compare our approach to existing compression techniques, such as quantization and pruning, and evaluate the trade-off between compression ratio and model accuracy. We will also investigate the application of our approach to real-world problems, such as image and language processing.
Risk Factors and Limitations: One potential risk of our approach is that the theoretical bounds we derive may not be tight, which could limit the practical applicability of our results. Additionally, our approach assumes a certain level of knowledge about the model architecture and the desired accuracy, which may not always be available in practice. Furthermore, the computational complexity of our approach may be high, which could limit its scalability to large models.
